key_points: All midshipmen live in Bancroft Hall, the largest single dormitory in the US
Zero choice in housing — assigned placement, no opt-out possible
Upperclassmen in your company directly control your daily life in the hall
key_quotes: The Company Officer worked 8 to 5, daily, and seldom interfaced with you - that was the job of the 1st Classmen
Had a classmate for four years who thought his shit didn't stink and let us all know it
My son is a Freshman (Plebe) and I visited him there for the 1st time over the weekend. What a place to go to college.

key_points: Grog bowl and mess night traditions create intense in-group bonding moments
Social conflict can turn personal fast — public ceremonies expose interpersonal tensions
Army-Navy game is a near-religious cultural event; rivalry defines identity here
key_quotes: someone made a foul. Instead of doing the punishment she went and chewed someone out for nomination
There will NEVER be any sports rivalry bigger than Army/Navy. Growing up as the son of a 34-year Navy veteran, that Saturday in December was like a religious holiday
I participated in two pranks that, to this day, have never been solved - nothing bad, just creative

key_points: King Hall feeds 4,400 midshipmen simultaneously — scale is staggering
13,500 meals per day; structured mandatory dining is part of military discipline
Peanut butter jar tradition sparks debate but reflects the culture of collective ritual
key_quotes: Yo guys chill damn, its not the first tradition that throws away food, and its not like the cost(a tiny portion of a peanut butter jar) is outclassed by the result
In the Army, we just ate the peanut butter. I suppose that's why we were better than the swabbys
I live in Annapolis and I'll see midshipman out and about with their computers. They're really serious about their studies and have very busy schedules

key_points: No matter how prepared you think you are for Plebe Summer — you aren't, per every grad
Academic pressure is extreme; top students suddenly find themselves in the bottom half
Medical separation means losing everything with no veterans benefits or healthcare
key_quotes: No matter how ready you 'think' you are for Plebe Summer and what follows --- YOU AREN'T!!
I am currently getting medically separated for two incurable diseases... I have also been told that I do not get veterans status or healthcare to treat my newly diagnosed disabilities
Sometimes I was happy as heck to be there, other times I wanted out. There are a lot of ups and downs but the key is to stay with it

key_points: Full ride — tuition, room, board, and stipend covered entirely, no debt
Compared directly to $71k/year Harvey Mudd; financial case is overwhelming
Service commitment is the real cost — 5 years minimum active duty post-graduation
key_quotes: Financials is critical in this decision, comparing a 71,000 a year school (harvey mudd) to a completely free, along with free study abroad and everything else school (naval academy)
I turned down Stanford yesterday to attend USNA. If finances are the only factor then your choice is clear, but also think about what you'll be giving up
I mean its not accurate to say someone got a full ride at West Point or Naval Academy…that's part of the package and they have to work to repay it

key_points: Calculus at USNA is dramatically harder than at civilian schools — don't coast on AP credit
Testing out of core classes can backfire; upperclassmen in your classes will target you
Faculty actively research; mids can collaborate, but teaching mission always comes first
key_quotes: I got a B+ in Calc I at the University of Southern California without studying much. Very different story with Calc I at USNA

I tested out of several classes to the point where I started on my major, immediately, and got targeted by the upperclassmen in my classes. They even reached into my company to see to it that I caught hell
USNA teachers as a rule make it work that way: the Mids come first. What we learn, we share.

key_points: DEI book removals (400+ titles) signal sharp political shift in institutional culture
Congressional nomination system makes geography matter as much as merit
Medical DQ process is opaque and can derail candidates late in the process
key_quotes: Naval Academy removes close to 400 books linked to DEI following DoD order
Because of the way the nomination process works, its 'easier' to get an appointment to the Naval Academy from states where there are not a lot of applicants
I got a letter of assurance but got rejected on grounds of medical disqualifications

key_points: Annapolis is a historic, walkable city — not a typical college town but genuinely special
Campus is a functioning military base, which restricts civilian access and student freedom
Proximity to DC and Baltimore gives real-world access; far better location than West Point
key_quotes: Downtown Annapolis feels firstly like a historic town where you're stepping back in time. Very family oriented as well as artsy. Then there's the unique atmosphere the Naval Academy brings — it's a college yet it's a military base
Annapolis is much closer to the city life and has a lot more to do than West Point
As someone born and raised in the Annapolis area I absolutely love what navy has brought and the strength I feel in that city

key_points: Only 7% of USNA grads make admiral — most commission and leave after 5 years
Engineering degree + military leadership opens strong civilian and federal career doors
NROTC at Duke produces the same commission for far more freedom — a real trade-off
key_quotes: O7 IS a 28 year commitment. 7% of USNA grads stick around. I'm not throwing that information around to discourage you from anything. I just want you to understand the timelines
I got out at 10 for grad school and did quite well
West Point is basically a free ticket to a top MBA

key_points: Best financial value of any elite school in America — literally zero cost with stipend
The 'cost' is 5+ years of service and total loss of lifestyle freedom during school
Medical separation with no veteran benefits is a catastrophic financial risk few discuss
key_quotes: comparing a 71,000 a year school (harvey mudd) to a completely free, along with free study abroad and everything else school (naval academy)
they have to work to repay it
I do not get veterans status or healthcare to treat my newly diagnosed disabilities

key_points: Intense shared suffering creates unusually deep lifelong bonds among classmates
You surrender individual autonomy completely — this is a feature for some, dealbreaker for others
Graduates are almost universally proud despite the difficulty; regret is rare
key_quotes: 34 years later and I still consider my plebe year classmates as close friends
20 years ago, I was recruited to row at the naval academy. At the last moment I decided on 'traditional college'. I wish to this day I had decided to go. If you get the chance, do it!
It kills a lot of bad habits and laziness that a regular college will let you get away with. Keeps you fit and helps build mental toughness.

key_points: Medical Policy: separation for illness means no vet status, no healthcare, no safety net
Upperclassman Culture: NAPS grads and legacy students form cliques that actively withhold help from peers
Political Climate: 400+ DEI books removed by DoD order; ideological environment shifting sharply
key_quotes: I do not get veterans status or healthcare to treat my newly diagnosed disabilities
Be wary of the guys/gals coming in with you from NAPS, they have a head start on you and won't help you because that would not make them superior
Legacy people are protected. I tested out of 5 classes and it helped, except I ended up in class with some upperclassmen who resented it

key_points: Only one cobbler on campus repairs gear for the entire brigade — a beloved institution
US Navy Band is world-class; musical culture at USNA is underappreciated externally
Summer Seminar is essentially a free week-long audition — use it to decide, not as a resume line
key_quotes: I was a hockey player at Navy, and when a new pair of gloves costs a month's pay, it could get pricey. Carlos in the cobbler shop would repair my gloves for me whenever I wore a hole in the palms

It's not an award or prestigious or something worth listing on your resume when applying to other schools. Think of it as a weeklong infomercial for USNA that you get to participate in so you can decide
I tested out of all the physics requirements. That gave me a head start and I got into a program that had me earning an MS in Aerospace Engineering (through U of MD) six months after graduating
